article_title: Stop using Cloudflare's default 1.1.1.1 DNS (changing one digit blocks malware at the router level)
core_thesis: Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1 DNS is fast and reliable but offers no security filtering; by simply changing one digit to 1.1.1.2, users gain automatic malware and phishing blocking at the DNS level, providing a passive, network-wide security layer that complements other defenses.
detailed_summary: The article explains that DNS servers act as the internet's phone book, translating domain names to IP addresses. While Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1 is popular for speed and privacy (via DNS over HTTPS), it does not filter out malicious domains. The author recommends switching to 1.1.1.2, which blocks known malware, phishing, and command-and-control domains by redirecting them to 0.0.0.0 (effectively a dead end). For stricter filtering, 1.1.1.3 also blocks adult content. The article acknowledges limitations: DNS filtering only works against known malicious domains and can produce false positives, so it should be part of a layered security approach. Setup instructions are provided for router-level (changing DNS in router settings) and per-device (Windows 10/11). The article also includes a quiz on networking basics and a comments section where users discuss alternatives like Pi-hole, Quad9, NextDNS, and self-hosted resolvers.

entities.name: Cloudflare
entities.role: Provider of public DNS resolvers 1.1.1.1, 1.1.1.2, and 1.1.1.3.

entities.name: Nick Lewis
entities.role: Author of the article, How-To Geek editor with programming and homelab experience.

entities.name: How-To Geek

entities.name: Quad9
entities.role: Alternative DNS provider mentioned in comments for privacy and malware blocking.

entities.name: Pi-hole
entities.role: Self-hosted DNS sinkhole mentioned in comments as an alternative to Cloudflare filtering.

entities.name: NextDNS
entities.role: DNS filtering service mentioned in comments.

entities.name: Technitium
entities.role: Self-hosted DNS server software mentioned in comments.

key_data_points: Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1 DNS does not block any domains; it resolves all queries including malicious ones. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: if you are simulating an attack, 1.1.1.1 will not interfere with your C2 infrastructure, but for defense, you want filtering.)

1.1.1.2 blocks known malware, phishing, and C2 domains by returning 0.0.0.0, preventing any connection. (🟢 For Red Teaming: this means your phishing or C2 domains must be unknown to Cloudflare's blocklist to succeed; for defense, this is a simple, set-and-forget layer.)
1.1.1.3 adds adult content blocking on top of malware filtering. (🟢 Useful for student environments or shared networks.)

DNS-based filtering only works against known malicious domains; new or compromised domains may bypass it. (🟢 Red Teaming: use domain generation algorithms or newly registered domains to evade such filters.)
Changing DNS at the router level applies the filter to all devices on the network. (🟢 Homelab: easy to implement on any router; can be combined with Pi-hole for additional filtering.)
The article provides step-by-step instructions for changing DNS on Windows and on routers (e.g., TP-Link). (🟢 Practical for student: quick to set up on personal devices.)

Comments highlight alternatives: Quad9 (9.9.9.9) for privacy, Pi-hole for self-hosted ad/malware blocking, NextDNS for customizable filtering, and Technitium for full control. (🟢 Homelab: these are more advanced options for those who want to self-host.)
methodology_steps: To change DNS at the router level: log into your router's admin interface (usually 192.168.0.1 or 10.0.0.1), navigate to DNS/DHCP settings, set Primary DNS to 1.1.1.2 and Secondary to 1.0.0.2, then save and reboot if necessary.

To change DNS on Windows 10/11: go to Settings > Network & internet > Ethernet (or Wi-Fi) > your network, click Edit next to DNS Server Assignment, change from Automatic to Manual, enable IPv4, set Preferred DNS to 1.1.1.2 and Alternative DNS to 1.0.0.2, then click Save.
For IPv6 users: use 2606:4700:4700::1112 (primary) and 2606:4700:4700::1002 (secondary) as noted in comments.
conclusions: Switching from 1.1.1.1 to 1.1.1.2 is a trivial change that adds a meaningful layer of protection against malware and phishing at no cost. (🟢 For Red Teaming: understand that many users will adopt this, so your phishing domains must be novel; for defense, implement this immediately.)

DNS filtering is not a silver bullet; it must be combined with other security measures like browser security settings, antivirus, and user awareness. (🟢 For student: this is a low-effort, high-impact security habit.)

The comments reveal a preference among tech-savvy users for self-hosted solutions (Pi-hole, Technitium) or privacy-focused providers (Quad9) over Cloudflare, indicating that advanced users may want more control. (🟢 Homelab: consider setting up Pi-hole or Technitium for granular filtering and logging.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your cybersecurity and homelab interests. As a Red Teamer, understanding DNS filtering helps you design attacks that bypass common blocklists (e.g., using new domains or encrypted DNS). For your homelab, you can implement 1.1.1.2 as a quick win or go further with Pi-hole/Technitium for custom filtering and logging. As a student, this is a simple, free way to add a security layer to your personal devices. The article also provides practical networking knowledge (DNS, DHCP, router configuration) that is foundational for both cybersecurity and IT.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a self-hosted DNS resolver like Technitium or Unbound with blocklists (e.g., from Firebog) for full control and privacy. This also gives you visibility into DNS queries, which is valuable for both Red Teaming (reconnaissance) and Blue Teaming (detection). If you want to test how your attack infrastructure fares against DNS filters, use a service like DNS Spy to check if your domains are blocked.
